lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The Hallmarks of Cancer
In the year 2000, two cancer biologists named Douglas Hanahan and Robert Weinberg sat down and tried to do something nobody had quite done before.
They tried to ask: across every kind of cancer — breast, lung, leukemia, melanoma, pancreas — what do all these wildly different diseases have in common?
Is there a list of capabilities, a sort of checklist, that any cell must acquire in order to become cancer?
And they wrote a paper, called simply "The Hallmarks of Cancer," that turned out to be one of the most cited papers in all of biology.
Because, yes — there is a list.
It's not infinite.
There are roughly half a dozen, maybe a dozen counting the updates, capabilities a cell has to acquire to become malignant.
And once you have that list, the whole field becomes navigable.
Every hallmark is something cancer cells do, and every hallmark is therefore something a drug might block.
Anatomy predicts pharmacology.
Let me walk you through the hallmarks, and for each one I'll give you a quick analogy.
We'll come back to all of them in more depth later — for now I just want you to have the menu.
Hallmark one: sustaining proliferative signaling.
Translation: the cell finds a way to keep telling itself to divide.
Imagine a thermostat that's stuck in the "on" position — it keeps shouting "heat the house!
" even when the house is already too hot.
Normal cells divide only when they receive specific external signals saying it's time.
Cancer cells generate those signals internally, or jam the receiver in the "on" position, so they're constantly hearing "divide, divide, divide.
Hallmark two: evading growth suppressors.
Translation: the cell ignores the signals telling it to stop.
Imagine driving a car where the brakes have been cut.
The brakes existed; they were doing their job; somebody disabled them.
We'll see in a few segments that breaking the brakes is, mechanically, very different from jamming the gas pedal, and the difference matters enormously for how we attack the disease.
Hallmark three: resisting cell death.
Your cells are programmed to commit suicide when something goes wrong — there's an actual built-in self-destruct program called apoptosis.
It's a beautiful safeguard.
Cancer cells have disabled the self-destruct.
The factory installed an emergency shutoff button on every machine; cancer cells have ripped the button out.
Hallmark four: enabling replicative immortality.
Normal cells can only divide a limited number of times before they exhaust a counter and stop.
Cancer cells reset the counter.
It's like a prepaid phone card that quietly refills itself.
Hallmark five: inducing angiogenesis.
Once a tumor gets bigger than about a millimeter — about the size of a sesame seed — the cells in the middle can't get enough oxygen or nutrients by diffusion.
They'd suffocate.
So tumors learn to send out chemical signals that recruit new blood vessels to grow into them, like a settlement laying its own water pipes.
No new pipes, no growth.
Hallmark six: activating invasion and metastasis.
Cancer cells learn to crawl out of their home tissue, get into the bloodstream or lymph, and set up colonies in distant organs.
This is the one that kills most patients.
A breast tumor in the breast is, in many cases, a manageable problem; a breast tumor that has set up shop in the liver and the bones and the brain is a very different problem.
Those are the original six.
Hanahan and Weinberg updated the list in 2011 and again in 2022, adding a few more.
Reprogramming energy metabolism.
Cancer cells rewire how they make ATP, often gulping glucose in a wasteful way.
We'll come back to why.
Evading immune destruction.
Your immune system actively patrols for abnormal cells and kills many cancers before you ever know they existed.
Successful cancers have figured out how to hide from, or actively disable, the immune patrol.
This is the hallmark that the entire immunotherapy revolution is built on.
Genome instability.
This one's a bit different — it's less a hallmark in itself and more an enabling hallmark.
Once a cell's DNA-repair machinery goes wonky, mutations accumulate faster, which lets the cell acquire all the other hallmarks faster.
It's a mutation that accelerates mutating.
Think of a printer that starts smudging the proofs; now every subsequent generation of paperwork has more typos.
Tumor-promoting inflammation.
Chronic inflammation in a tissue can actually fertilize the soil for cancer.
The immune system's healing signals get co-opted by the tumor for its own growth.
So that's the hallmark framework.
Don't try to memorize the list right now.
Just hold this idea: becoming cancer is not one thing.
It's a stack of capabilities, acquired one by one over years or decades, each one nudging the cell further away from cooperative behavior and closer to autonomous, parasitic growth.
And every capability on the list is, in principle, a target for a drug.
